derivation: A block is the unit of transfer between memory and secondary storage; you cannot read less than one block, so it is the SMALLEST amount accessed at a time. C is wrong because a block normally holds several records (blocking factor).
Logical write = move a record into the program buffer; physical write = actually transfer the buffer out to the external file/device. Statement matches physical write, so True (option A).

Stem reads as a verbatim course definition of the blocking/deblocking routine sitting between file and program: read one block, hand the program one record at a time. Strictly the split direction is de-blocking, which is the only reason to doubt True, but the intended answer is True (option A).
Odd parity sets the parity bit so the TOTAL count of 1 bits is odd. The stem describes even parity, so the statement is false. Here False is option A (labels are reversed vs the usual order).

A secondary key is any non-primary-key field used as an access key to retrieve records. A and B read 'secondary' positionally (second key / second field) and are wrong. C's wording is slightly garbled ('key to another file' likely OCR of 'key to a file') but is the only option expressing the real definition.
Windows and DOS ARE operating systems, so the negative claim 'are not Operating Systems' is false. False is option A here.

An OS is system software, not application software; it is the platform application software runs on. Statement is False = option B. (Item is flagged as a corrected/derived key; my independent answer matches the corrected key, not the printed one.)

Classic waterfall SDLC order: requirement analysis -> specification -> design -> coding -> testing. The first two phases are requirement analysis and specification (A); every other option leads with design or coding.

A programming language is defined by syntax (legal form) and semantics (meaning). D ('Syntax and Language Rules') is redundant since language rules are syntax; commands/procedures are things written IN a language, not components OF one.

A computer program is a detailed set of instructions telling the computer what to do (A). B is an artificially narrow subset ('only read files and print reports') and is not a valid definition, which kills 'Both 1 and 2'.

The ALU is the specific unit that performs elementary arithmetic and logic operations (add, subtract, AND, OR, compare). CPU is the containing unit (ALU + control unit + registers), too broad for 'the device that performs' the operations.
printf and scanf are C standard library functions that remain fully usable in C++ via <cstdio>. 'Reads' is not a C++ command at all, so it is the odd one out (A).

The stack pointer holds the ADDRESS of the top of the stack, not data. The register holding data currently being worked on is the accumulator / general-purpose register. Statement is False = option B.
